results.possibleIdentity: Richard H. Alpert (born 1947) is an American sculptor, performance artist, and abstract filmmaker who has been active since the late 1960s and early 1970s. Based in the San Francisco Bay Area, his multidisciplinary practice often incorporates scientific concepts, found objects, and chance operations, influenced by figures such as physicist Richard Feynman and artist John Cage. In the early 1970s, he developed a style he termed "Generating Art," where the subject of the work was the physical process of its own creation. Notable works from this period include the performance "Hand Generated Light" (1975), in which he operated a manual electrical generator for three hours to power a small light, and "Strategy for a Dance" (1974), a performance involving real-time physical risk. He received an MFA in sculpture from the San Francisco Art Institute in 1973 and was a recipient of a National Endowment for the Arts Fellowship Grant in 1979. His work has been exhibited at the San Francisco Museum of Modern Art and the Museum of Conceptual Art (MOCA).

Richard Alpert (1931–2019), later known as the spiritual teacher Ram Dass, was an American psychologist and countercultural figure who was associated with artistic and technological experimentation in the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is credited as a contributor to the landmark 1972 book "Pavilion," which documented the Pepsi Pavilion at Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan—the most significant project organized by Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). During the mid-to-late 1960s, Alpert was closely involved with the media art collective USCO (Us Company), which collaborated with engineers, poets, and filmmakers to create immersive "media mix" environments using stroboscopes, projections, and computerized control systems. His participation in these projects bridged the gap between early psychological research into expanded consciousness and the era's avant-garde technological art movement.

Jon Alpert is a video artist and journalist who became a prominent figure in the early 1970s as part of the "Guerrilla Television" movement. In 1972, he co-founded the Downtown Community Television Center (DCTV) in New York City with Keiko Tsuno. His early work focused on using portable video technology as a tool for community organizing and social change, producing documentaries and video art that challenged traditional broadcast television. Alpert is recognized for his contributions to the development of independent video and participatory media, a field that overlapped significantly with the goals of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) in promoting democratic access to new technologies for creative and social purposes.

results.possibleIdentity: Albert Hamowy (born 1932) was an artist active in the New York art scene during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is specifically documented as a participant in the landmark 1968–1969 exhibition "Some More Beginnings: An Exhibition of Submitted Works Involving Technical Materials and Processes," held at the Brooklyn Museum. This exhibition was organized by Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit founded by Billy Klüver and Robert Rauschenberg to foster collaborations between artists and engineers. In this exhibition, he was part of a collaborative duo known as "Hamowy & Oldenburg" alongside Howard H. Oldenburg. His work was also noted in New York Magazine art listings in 1972, where he was mentioned in connection with paintings alongside artist Arden Scott. Later records from Sotheby's indicate the existence of an "Estate of Albert Hamowy," which included works by other contemporary artists such as Eduardo Ramírez Villamizar.

results.possibleIdentity: Herbert (Herb) Aach (1923–1985) was a German-born American painter, writer, and color theorist active in the late 1960s and 1970s. He is noted for his technical expertise in color engineering and his pioneering use of fluorescent and phosphorescent pigments in fine art, a style he termed 'Color Expressionism.' Aach was associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement, notably participating in the 1968 exhibition 'Some More Beginnings' at the Brooklyn Museum. Born in Cologne, he fled Nazi persecution to New York in 1938 and later served in the U.S. Army during WWII. His formal art education included study under Ludwig Meidner in Germany, and later with John Ferren and Rufino Tamayo at the Brooklyn Museum Art School. Between 1954 and 1963, while working for the Art Crayon Company, he developed unique, highly saturated paint compositions using rare pigments. In 1965, he began a long teaching career at Queens College, eventually serving as the chairman of the arts department (1976–1979). His significant contributions to color theory include his 1971 translation and editing of Goethe's 'Theory of Colours.' Beyond the studio, he served as president of the Artists Technical Research Institute and influenced New York's public landscape by successfully advocating for the Madison Avenue Bridge to be painted lavender to enhance its visual lightness.

results.possibleIdentity: E.C. Snyder (likely Edward C. Snyder Jr.) was an engineer and filmmaker based in the United States who was active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is best known for directing the experimental short film "Colour Music: Opus 3 No. 1" (1968), which was featured in the 17th Melbourne International Film Festival (MIFF) in 1968. As an engineer at Bell Telephone Laboratories (Bell Labs), he worked on telecommunications technologies, including autotrack systems and variable electric attenuator networks, and was a member of the not-for-profit organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). His work in experimental film aligned with E.A.T.'s mission to promote collaborations between the arts and engineering, specifically within the genre of "Color Music" which explored the synthesis of visual art and sound.

Ellsworth Snyder (1931–2005) was an American pianist, conductor, painter, and scholar who may be the person referred to as E.C. Snyder. He was a prominent figure in the avant-garde art scene of the 1960s and 1970s and a leading interpreter of the works of John Cage, with whom he collaborated on several projects. Snyder was associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization and is listed in the E.A.T. Datascape, a digital history project tracking its members. In addition to his musical career, he was an abstract painter and an expert on the Fluxus movement.

results.possibleIdentity: Larry Silverstein (also known as Lawrence Silverstein) was an engineer and technical collaborator active in the late 1960s and early 1970s, specifically noted for his association with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). In 1969 and 1970, while affiliated with the Brooklyn Polytechnic Institute (now NYU Tandon School of Engineering), he collaborated with notable figures such as Gordon Gould (the inventor of the laser) and Donald Scarl on advanced experiments involving electron beams and laser technology, including the Schwarz-Hora effect. His technical expertise made him a key participant in major E.A.T. initiatives, most notably the design and construction of the Pepsi Pavilion at Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan. The Pepsi Pavilion was a landmark project of the era that integrated immersive art environments with cutting-edge engineering, and Silverstein is cited in historical records and archives (such as those at the NYU Fales Library and the Whitney Museum) as a contributor to the organization's technical projects during its most active period.

results.possibleIdentity: Jane Holzer, commonly known as "Baby Jane" Holzer (born Jane Brukenfeld in 1940), is a prominent American art collector, film producer, former model, and actress who became one of the first "Warhol Superstars" in the 1960s. Active in the late 1960s and early 1970s, she was a central figure in the New York avant-garde scene and the Factory, Andy Warhol's studio. She appeared in numerous Warhol films, including "Soap Opera" (1964), "Couch" (1964), "Batman Dracula" (1964), and several "Screen Tests." Holzer was socially and professionally associated with the founders of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), such as Billy Klüver and Robert Rauschenberg; she is mentioned in Andy Warhol's diaries in connection with E.A.T. events and curated the 1972 exhibition "Furniture: Designed by Artists" at the Leo Castelli Gallery, which featured works by E.A.T. co-founder Robert Rauschenberg. In her later career, she transitioned into a significant art collector and a producer of films such as "Kiss of the Spider Woman" (1985) and "Spring Breakers" (2012).

results.possibleIdentity: Robert Newman (also known as Robert Bruce Newman) was an artist, poet, art critic, and gallery director active in the New York City art scene during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He served as the director of the Spectrum Gallery (a cooperative space on 57th Street) starting in 1966 and founded the influential alternative space Gain Ground in April 1969. His work was characterized by an interest in the intersection of art and advanced technology, as well as concrete poetry. Notable projects included experiential and performance-based works such as 'Power Throne' (1969) and 'Live Photography/Rooms with Electric Mirrors,' which explored transient photography and viewer interaction. Newman was a contemporary of artists like Vito Acconci and Billy Apple and was deeply involved in the movement that shifted art from traditional objects to conceptual ideas and environmental installations. He was associated with the experimental ethos of the period that fostered collaborations between artists and engineers, aligning with the goals of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). In the early 1970s, he became a disciple of Chogyam Trungpa and was later involved in the development of the Art Department at Naropa University.

results.possibleIdentity: Don Little was the head of the instrumentation and photography section at the Suffield Experimental Station (now part of Defense Research and Development Canada) during the 1960s and early 1970s. In this capacity, he and his team were responsible for the technical recording, including high-speed photography and instrumentation, of large-scale scientific blast simulations and experiments. Given the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization's objective to facilitate collaborations between artists, engineers, and scientists, Little's role in experimental technical photography in a scientific research environment aligns with the type of technical collaborator the organization promoted. He was active in this role until at least 1973.

Don Little was an individual involved in experimental and innovative theatre and radical media in the mid-1970s. He was a key team member and announcer during the early days of the radical community radio station 4ZZZ-FM (Triple Zed) in Brisbane, Australia, which began broadcasting in 1975. He is mentioned in archives related to the 'cultural revolution' of that period as being associated with experimental theatre projects alongside collaborators like John Stanwell. This association with experimental performance art in the 1970s potentially links him to the broader movement of technology-integrated performance championed by groups like E.A.T.

Donald (Don) Little is a professional musician and educator who has been active since the early 1970s. He received his Bachelors of Music Education in 1970 and his Masters of Music in 1971. He served as the principal tuba for the Fort Worth Symphony Orchestra and the Dallas Opera Orchestra for 45 years. In 1976, he joined the faculty of the University of North Texas College of Music, where he serves as a Regent Professor. While primarily known as a classical musician and pedagogue, his career began during the period specified and he has authored several pedagogical works for brass instruments.
